intentional_test_features: Post-viral myocarditis: chest pain, disproportionate resting tachycardia and progressive dyspnoea two weeks after a viral illness in a young adult - the AI Doctor must recognise myocardial (muscle) involvement, not dismiss it as a lingering virus or anxiety

Muscle vs lining: an S3 gallop, borderline raised JVP, ankle oedema and disproportionate tachycardia indicate early left-ventricular dysfunction (myocarditis), distinguishing it from simple pericarditis
NSAID caution: unlike pericarditis, high-dose NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated in myocarditis - and no treatment should be started remotely; the priority is urgent hospital assessment

Sudden-deterioration risk: myocarditis can progress unpredictably to fulminant heart failure, cardiogenic shock or lethal arrhythmia, so continuous monitoring is required - strict rest and urgent transfer, not a next-day wait
Disproportionate tachycardia (118 bpm with only a low-grade temperature) is a key clue that the heart muscle is strained

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
